coredata.dc:title: Considering Battery Degradation in Life Cycle Greenhouse Gas Emission Analysis of Electric Vehicles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:issueName: 25th CIRP Life Cycle Engineering (LCE) Conference, 30 April – 2 May 2018, Copenhagen, Denmark
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Fan
Xie, Yuanyuan
Deng, Yelin
Yuan, Chris
coredata.dc:description: Lithium ion batteries (LIBs) are predominantly employed to power Electric Vehicles (EVs). Battery degradation can significantly affect battery performance and further influence the energy consumptions and life cycle greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from EVs. However, currently battery degradation has not been taken into account in life cycle assessment (LCA) of EVs. In this paper, we report our research on considering battery degradation into life cycle GHG emissions of EVs, by analyzing a mid-size EV using a 24 kWh Lithium-Manganese-Oxide (LMO)-graphite battery. Both cycling loss and calendar loss in battery degradation are analyzed under average driving conditions of U.S. The model is validated with experimental testing data on Nissan Leaf from Argonne National Lab. In this study, the required battery replacement is also integrated into the LCA GHG emission model and results, by considering the cradle-to-gate GHG emissions from a battery pack. This model can improve the accuracy and reliability of life cycle GHG studies on EVs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: battery degradation
life cycle

electric vehicle

head.ce:title: Considering Battery Degradation in Life Cycle Greenhouse Gas Emission Analysis of Electric Vehicles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, 44106, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, 44106

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Science and Engineering, Argonne National Laboratory, Argonne, 60439, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Science and Engineering, Argonne National Laboratory, Argonne, 60439

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215006

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium ion batteries (LIBs) are predominantly employed to power Electric Vehicles (EVs). Battery degradation can significantly affect battery performance and further influence the energy consumptions and life cycle greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from EVs. However, currently battery degradation has not been taken into account in life cycle assessment (LCA) of EVs. In this paper, we report our research on considering battery degradation into life cycle GHG emissions of EVs, by analyzing a mid-size EV using a 24 kWh Lithium-Manganese-Oxide (LMO)-graphite battery. Both cycling loss and calendar loss in battery degradation are analyzed under average driving conditions of U.S. The model is validated with experimental testing data on Nissan Leaf from Argonne National Lab. In this study, the required battery replacement is also integrated into the LCA GHG emission model and results, by considering the cradle-to-gate GHG emissions from a battery pack. This model can improve the accuracy and reliability of life cycle GHG studies on EVs.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: USEPA., Inventory of U.S. Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Sinks: 1990-2012. 2014, U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
